title_en: Taharah in Al-Muqni': Comprehensive Hanbali Purity Law
title_ar: الطهارة في المقنع: أحكام الطهارة الحنبلية الشاملة
content_en:
The taharah chapter of Al-Muqni' presents Hanbali purity law at a level of detail that goes beyond the primers while remaining more accessible than the encyclopedic Al-Mughni. Ibn Qudamah's approach here is characteristic of the intermediate tier: enough detail to give students a complete picture, with occasional glimpses of the evidential reasoning that the advanced works explore in full.

On water, Al-Muqni' states the categories clearly and adds detail on specific questions that primers cannot address. The ruling on water in which a dead animal has fallen, on well water and how to purify it if something dies in it, and on the water used to clean an impure vessel are all addressed. Ibn Qudamah's treatment is practical: he focuses on cases that students and practitioners will actually encounter, not merely theoretical classifications.

The chapter on wudu in Al-Muqni' covers not only the obligatory elements and nullifiers but also the recommended practices in a way that gives students a complete picture of the ideal wudu and the minimum valid wudu. The Hanbali obligation to maintain tartib (sequence) and muwalah (continuity) — the two elements that distinguish Hanbali wudu from Hanafi wudu, which does not require sequence — is presented with enough explanation for students to understand why it matters.

Ibn Qudamah's treatment of the masah 'ala al-khuffayn (wiping over footwear) is particularly thorough in Al-Muqni'. He covers the conditions for valid wiping — the footwear must have been put on in a state of ritual purity, must cover the area required to be washed, must be normally wearable — the time limits, the conditions that end the validity of wiping (removal of the footwear, completion of the permitted time period, requiring ghusl), and the rulings on wiping over casts and bandages. This section reflects the practical importance of these rulings in daily life.

The chapter on removing najasah is comprehensive, covering the types of najasah, the difference between severe najasah (requiring seven washings for dog and pig) and other types, the ruling on small amounts of blood or pus that are excused, and the conditions under which stains that cannot be completely removed are excused. Ibn Qudamah presents these rulings in a way that equips students to handle the common questions of daily life.

On the purification of the ground through drying by sunlight and wind — a point on which the Hanbali school allows purification without washing in certain cases — Al-Muqni' states the conditions carefully. This reflects the Hanbali school's practical concern for situations where washing a large area (such as a courtyard) is impractical.
